coredata.dc:title: Origin of solvent and excitation dependent emission in newly synthesized amphiphilic carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Singhal, Pallavi
Vats, Bal Govind
Pulhani, Vandana
coredata.dc:description: The present work demonstrates the synthesis of a new amphiphilic carbon dot from octylamine. Steady-state emission studies show excitation dependent emission features. Time-resolved emission studies show three different emission decay traces while exciting at 267 nm, 292–339 nm, and 374–445 nm regions. These excitation bands are assigned to the π–π*, n–π*, and surface group states respectively. It was further observed that only surface state exhibit excitation dependent emission behavior while both π–π* and n–π* states show marginal effect; the observation rarely discussed in the literature. Both growth and emission lifetime of π–π* is higher as compared to n–π* and surface group states suggesting the possibility of energy transfer from former to latter. The emission peak position was different in different solvents by exciting π–π* states however it was almost same when monitoring at the surface states which in contrast to the belief that π–π* states are embedded inside the shell and has the least exposure to the solvent. The study also suggests that in protic solvents both π–π* and n–π* states interact but not in other solvents. Moreover, the presence of multiple emission states is responsible for excitation dependent emission features from surface group states, each having its own lifetime.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Amphiphilic
Octylamine
Time resolved emission
Solvent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Steady state absorption (A) and emission spectra of CDs (B).
(A) XRD, (B) FTIR, (C) TEM and, (D) HRTEM of CDs.
Excitation wavelength dependent steady state emission spectra on CDs in chloroform.
Spectral dependence of the PL emission maximum as a function of the excitation wavelength.
Time resolved emission decay traces of CDs by exciting at different wavelengths and monitoring the emission at 400, 450 and 500 nm in chloroform, propanol and ethyl acetate.
Time resolved emission decay traces of CDs by exciting at 267, 292, 339, 374, 402 and 445 nm and monitoring the emission at 450 and 500 nm in various solvents.
Time resolved emission decay traces of CDs by exciting at 292, 339 and 402 nm and monitoring the emission at different wavelengths in chloroform, 2-propanol and ethyl acetate.
Different excitation and emission processes involved in CDs.

head.ce:title: Origin of solvent and excitation dependent emission in newly synthesized amphiphilic carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Monitoring and Assessment Division Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, 400085, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Environmental Monitoring and Assessment Division Bhabha Atomic Research Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental Monitoring and Assessment Division Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, 400085, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fuel Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, 400085, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fuel Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work demonstrates the synthesis of a new amphiphilic carbon dot from octylamine. Steady-state emission studies show excitation dependent emission features. Time-resolved emission studies show three different emission decay traces while exciting at 267 nm, 292–339 nm, and 374–445 nm regions. These excitation bands are assigned to the π–π*, n–π*, and surface group states respectively. It was further observed that only surface state exhibit excitation dependent emission behavior while both π–π* and n–π* states show marginal effect; the observation rarely discussed in the literature. Both growth and emission lifetime of π–π* is higher as compared to n–π* and surface group states suggesting the possibility of energy transfer from former to latter. The emission peak position was different in different solvents by exciting π–π* states however it was almost same when monitoring at the surface states which in contrast to the belief that π–π* states are embedded inside the shell and has the least exposure to the solvent. The study also suggests that in protic solvents both π–π* and n–π* states interact but not in other solvents. Moreover, the presence of multiple emission states is responsible for excitation dependent emission features from surface group states, each having its own lifetime.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) constitute an important class of luminescent materials and have drawn increasing attention due to their fascinating properties, such as low cost, high photostability, tunable excitation and emission, low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility [1–9]. These properties make these dots a fascinating candidate over semiconductor quantum dots [10–16] and ﬂuorescent dyes [17,18] to study their applications in fields such as; bioimaging [19], sensing [3], homogeneous catalysis, photocatalyst design, fabrication of composite materials, and optoelectronic devices [1,4,5,20,21]. To date, a large number of CDs have been successfully prepared from both “top−down” and “bottom−up” strategies but most of these methods synthesize hydrophilic dots thereby limiting their applications in areas where it is necessary that the dots are organophilic [3,17,20,22,23]. Few of these areas are fabrication of composite materials and optoelectronic devices. Here it is important to mention that the applications utilizing both hydrophilic and hydrophobic CDs can be combined if these properties are present in a single system. For this purpose, amphiphilic CDs are attaining significant attention nowadays and few studies have been carried out on their synthesis [1,2,24]. In this line, Zhou et al. [1] have reported the synthesis of amphibious fluorescent CDs from natural polysaccharide. Wang et al. [25] have shown the synthesis of amphiphilic CDs from egg and use them as printing ink. Kozak et al. [7] have synthesized CDs from cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium chloride. Choi et al. [6] have synthesized amphiphilic CDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine. Wang et al. [5] have synthesized amphiphilic CDs by conjugating hydrophobic alkyl epoxide to the surface amino groups of PEI 600-derived CDs and use it for drug delivery and bio-imaging. It suggests that few studies have been carried out on synthesis of amphiphilic CDs but the field is in nascent stage. The work carried out in present study adds to it and demonstrate a new method for the synthesis of amphiphilic CDs.

It is well known that most of the application involving CDs utilize its emission properties which although is a highly explored feature still not clearly understood. It is observed that many of the dots exhibit excitation dependent emission behaviour and a number of studies were carried out to explain this feature [3,26–29]. Among these the most common and widely appreciated explanations are the distribution of different emissive sites on each CDs surface [28], abundance of carboxyl functional groups on the surface coupled with the competition between various emission centres, and the particle size distribution [27,28,30]. Recently few in depth studies have been carried out to understand the origin of this feature in detail. In this line, Sharma et al. [8,9] have synthesized CDs from citric acid and urea and observed that aggregation is one of the most important phenomenon and the engrossment of multiple discrete electronic states due to the presence of different types of aggregates leads to huge excitation dependent shifting of emission spectra of CDs. Fu et al. [31] have observed that the excitation dependent emission behaviour of CDs arise from a selective excitation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon with slightly different energy gaps and energy transfer between them. Khan et al. [26] have shown that the emission spectra of CDs is broadened because of slower relaxation of solvent molecules around it and give rise to excitation dependent emission. Sun and co-workers [32] have highlighted the involvement of trap states in excitation dependent emission properties which has been supported by other research groups [33,34] also. Krysmann et al. [35] have synthesized CDs at two different temperatures and observed that the formation temperature has a major effect on emission properties where low temperature favours the formation of molecular fluorophore while at high temperature formation of carbogenic core is favourable. In many studies, contributions from polarity, pH, surface passivation, etc. toward the excitation dependence or independence have also been established. It suggests that a number of studies has been carried out to understand the emission properties of CDs still the field is open and a lot of work is needed in this direction.

In our present work, we have synthesized a new amphiphilic CDs and study its emission behaviour. The synthetic parameters; such as time and temperature were varied to obtain the optimum emission and understand the effect of these parameters. Study was performed in different solvents to understand the effect of polarity on emission behaviour and a possible reason for excitation dependent emission behaviour is explored.
Octylamine (OA) was purchased from Aldrich and used without further purification. High purity solvents were used throughout the preparation.

CDs were synthesized by taking OA as the precursor. For this, 10 ml of OA was taken in a beaker and heated for 1 h at 100 °C in open air. This results in a residue which was dissolved into chloroform. The same procedure was followed for 2 h and at 150 and 200 °C temperatures. The samples were named as CDs-100:1 (Δ100 °C for 1hr), CDs-100:2 (Δ100 °C for 2hr), CDs-150:1 (Δ150 °C for 1hr), CDs-150:2 (Δ150 °C for 2hr), and CDs-200:1 (Δ200 °C for 1hr). The compounds were purified by dialyzing using 1 K membrane for 24 h.

As mentioned that CDs can be an alternative for organic dyes and semiconductor QDs because of their unique emission characteristics; steady state absorption and emission studies were carried out to understand these features. Fig. 1 shows the steady state absorption and emission spectra of CDs at different synthetic conditions. It is clearly observed from Fig. 1A that the absorption spectra of the synthesized dots are different suggesting the effect of temperature and time on the absorption behavior. It is to be noted that absorption spectra cover a range from 240 to 500 nm but with different transition probability. Based on the transition modes, the spectrum is divided into three regions. One is absorption centered at 243 nm, second is an absorption band from 290 to 350 nm regions and third is a continuous absorption band beyond 350 nm. Here the band centered close to 243 nm has been assigned to the core state (π–π*) transition from the aromatic sp [2] domains (CC). While band from 290 to 350 nm comes from defect states (n–π*), which mainly include oxygenous functional groups C–O and CO. Finally, a third band beyond 350 nm is assigned to the surface group transition. It is interesting to observe that although in these CDs all three bands are present but the intensity ratio of the band varies which suggest different number of functional groups on each CDs surface and hence the effect of synthesis conditions on CDs absorption property. To further analyze this, steady state emission studies were carried out by exciting the samples at 360 nm and are shown in Fig. 1B. It is interesting to observe from Fig. 1B that there is a slight decrement in emission intensity as reaction temperature or time increases. This suggests that low reaction temperature and time favors the formation of more emissive CDs in the present case. Henceforth for further studies we have chosen CDs-100:1 and hereafter it will be abbreviated as CDs.

Structure illustration of CDs was carried out and is shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2A depict the XRD pattern where a broad hump at 25° was observed which signifies the presence of CDs [3]. FTIR studies were carried out to determine the functional groups and are shown in Fig. 2B. A clear –NH stretch can be seen at 3300 cm−1. A weak –CO carboxylic acid stretch comes at 1753 cm−1 and a strong –CO amide stretch is seen at 1668 cm−1. This shows the presence of mainly amide group at the surface of CDs along with some COOH groups. We have also taken FTIR spectra of OA where no such features were observed and are shown in Fig. S1. TEM studies were carried out which shows that the size of the CDs is ∼10 nm (Fig. 2C). HRTEM study shows that the dots are crystalline in nature and are shown in Fig. 2D. To further illustrate the structure we have performed NMR analysis and are shown in Fig. S2. 13C NMR of CDs shows major peaks in the range δ = 13–40 ppm which are due to the aliphatic carbons (like –CH2 and –CH3) present in the structure of CDs. Peaks at δ = 134 and 147 ppm signifies the presence of.

C=C bonds in the structure. A small peak at δ = 161 ppm is due to CO moiety present in the surface. 1H NMR spectra of CDs shows peaks at δ = 0.8–8.2 ppm which describe the presence of aliphatic, alkene as well as aromatic protons in the backbone of CDs. Since OA at 100 °C cannot form CDs we believe that the formation of dots might occur from the impurities present in the OA. Although we could not find out the impurities but short chain unsaturated impurities present in OA might lead to the formation of CDs.

As CDs show three different bands in the absorption spectra, numerous studies have been carried out in past which explain the absorption feature of CDs, but different opinions exist in this. In this line Sharma et al. [8] have monitored the absorption behavior of CDs and observed two bands at 220 and 350 nm and assigned it to the π–π* and n–π* transitions respectively. The same group [9] has later shown that the band at 350 nm has contribution from both π–π* and n–π* states. Yu et al. [36] have synthesized CDs using acetic acid as one of the precursor and observed two bands in the absorption spectra. The band at 250 nm is assigned to the π−π* transition of aromatic CC bonds, while a shoulder at 300 nm is attributed to the n–π* transition of CO bonds. Wang et al. [4] have synthesized CDs from citric acid and ionic liquid and observed the absorption band from 300 to 400 nm which was assigned to the absorptions of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Zhou et al. [1] have synthesized CDs from natural polysaccharide and observed an absorption band at 340 nm and assign it to the aromatic π orbitals. This suggests that different description exist to explain the absorption feature in CDs still the actual origin remains debatable. In our present study we try to address this feature.

Excitation wavelength dependent steady state emission studies were carried out in CDs and are shown in Fig. 3 . It is clearly seen from Fig. 3 that the synthesized CDOT exhibit excitation dependent emission behavior. This behavior is in contrast to other inorganic.

Semiconductors where quantum confinement leads to excitation independent emission [10–16] hence violating Kasha−Vavilov rule [37]. Similar feature has been observed earlier also [8,9,17,36] by many researchers. Also it is observable from Fig. 3 that excitation in 260–310 nm range shows a hump ∼350 nm along with a broad band at ∼440 nm. Excitation in 320–380 nm range shows a band ∼410–440 nm with maximum emission intensity at 435 nm following 370 nm excitation. Excitation beyond 400 nm shift the emission band to the red region with a decrease in emission intensity. This excitation dependent emission feature in CDs has been explained by different mechanisms. Among these the distribution of different emissive sites on each CDs surface [28], the abundance of carboxyl functional groups on the surface, and the particle size distribution [27,28,30] are mostly accepted. In addition to this, other explanations are also proposed. In this line, Sharma et al. [9] have shown that aggregation induced spectral splitting is the reason for excitation dependent emission feature. Khan et al. [26] have shown that such feature arise because of the slower relaxation of the solvent molecules around CDs. Fu et al.31 have shown that CDs exist as a system comprised different types of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) embedded in a polymer matrix and selective excitation of PAHs is the reason for excitation dependent emission. Strauss et al. [28] have suggested that the short wavelength emission in CDs can be explained by the intrinsic luminescence from sp [2]-carbon networks, but other deactivation pathways dominate the luminescence at longer wavelengths. Ghosh et al. [34] have suggested that CDs behave as an electric dipoles, and their emission originates from the recombination of photogenerated charges on defect centres involving a strong coupling between the electronic transition and collective vibrations of the lattice structure. It suggests that different mechanism has been proposed for this peculiar feature in CDs still not a single mechanism is accepted worldwide. In our present work we try to explore the reason behind this excitation dependent emission behaviour by carrying out experiments in different polar and non polar solvents and time resolved emission measurements.

Steady state absorption spectra of CDs in various polar and non polar solvents are recorded and shown in Fig. S2. As mentioned above that the absorption spectra of CDs shows both π–π* and n–π* bands, a change in solvent polarity should ideally change the absorption position of these bands. Though, such features could not be determined in this study as clear bands comprising these transitions are not seen. To discuss the effect of solvent polarity on the emission properties, steady state emission spectra was recorded and is shown in Fig. S3. It is interesting to observe from Fig. S3 that in all the solvents CDs shows excitation dependent emission feature and the emission position does not change much with change in solvent polarity. To illustrate it, we have plotted emission peak position as a function of excitation wavelength in various solvents and shown in Fig. 4 . It is observed from Fig. 4 that in 300–350 nm excitation regions, emission wavelength is independent of excitation wavelength in chloroform, and toluene while a weak dependence is observed in other solvents following which a continuous excitation dependent emission behavior is observed in all solvents. As mentioned above that transition in 290–350 nm region is assigned to the n–π* and >350 nm region is assigned to the surface group transitions, the results herein suggest that the emission originating from n–π* transitions are almost independent of excitation wavelength while emission from surface group transitions are strongly dependent on excitation wavelength suggesting the presence of multiple excitation and emission states. Also in case of surface group transitions the emission peak position is almost same in all the solvents which suggests that the solvent relaxation is slow as compared to intraband relaxation and therefore not the reason for excitation dependent emission behavior in contrast to mentioned by various research groups [7,9,26,38–40]. This feature will subsequently be discussed in time resolved emission studies.

Fig. 5 shows the time resolved emission decay traces of CDs by exciting the samples at 267, 292, 339, 374, and 402 nm and monitoring the emission at 400, 450 and 500 nm in chloroform, propanol and ethylacetate. Similar experiments were also carried out in other solvents and shown in Fig. S4. Following observations can be drawn from Fig. 5 and Fig. S4. (1) Three different emission decay traces were observed which corresponds to three excitation states; 267 nm, 292–339 nm and 374–402 nm. It is observable from Fig. 5 that the emission originating from these three states are different at all three emission wavelengths. This clearly shows that the CDs have three different excitation states and the emission lifetime of each of these states is different. These states has been assigned to the π–π*, n–π* and surface group states. An exception to this is propanol where emission decay trace is same at 267 and 292–339 nm excitations when emission is monitored at 400 nm. Similar features were also observed in methanol when emission is monitored at 370 nm while exciting at 267 and 292–339 nm (Fig. S4). This suggests mixing of both π–π* and n–π* states in protic solvents. We have calculated the emission lifetime of CDs and given in Table S1. It is seen from Table S1 that the emission lifetime of CDs at 292 and 339 ex is almost same when the emission is monitored at 400 nm. The same is true at 450 and 500 nm emission. Here it is important to recall from steady state emission studies that the emission peak position is independent of excitation wavelength in 300–350 nm region, our time resolved emission decay experiments further shows that the emission lifetime is independent of excitation energy in this region and emission occur from same state. (2) Time resolved emission decay trace is same at 374 and 402 nm excitations, although in steady state emission studies we have observed excitation dependent emission behavior in this region. This suggests that the emission lifetime of surface group states is independent of excitation energy. (3) As the excitation wavelength proceed toward Blue region, both growth and decay time increases irrespective of the solvent. It shows that the emission lifetime is more in π–π* transitions followed by n–π* and surface group transitions. Higher lifetime of π–π* states suggests the possibility of energy transfer and the charge carriers can move to n–π* and subsequently to surface group states before their emission from π–π* and n–π* states. Also higher growth time of π–π* states as compared to n–π* and surface group states further compliment it. Here it is noteworthy from steady state emission studies that the maximum emission occurs from surface groups even after exciting π–π* and n–π* states, which further suggest the energy transfer possibility.

To analyze the effect of solvent on emission decay traces, time resolved emission studies were carried out and are shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S5. It is interesting to observe from Fig. 6 and Fig. S5 that the emission decay trace does not change with change in polarity of the solvent while exciting at 339, 374, 402 and 445 nm. As mentioned above that these states corresponds to the n–π* and surface group transitions states, it shows that the solvent polarity does not have any effect on the emission lifetime from these states and intraband relaxation within the states is much faster as compared to solvent relaxation. This is in contrast to the work suggested by Khan et al. [26] where they have shown the solvent relaxation leads to excitation dependent emission in CDs. A closer look at Fig. 6 and Fig. S5 suggest that once the samples are excited at 267 nm which is considered to be the transitions belonging to π– π* states, the solvent has profound effect when the emission is monitored at 370 and 400 nm. It has been suggested that the blue region (∼240–300 nm) comprises of core state belonging to π– π*

Transitions and are embedded inside the shell of surface groups. However it has been shown here that the solvent very well affect both steady state and time resolved emission properties once the emission is recorded from these states. This clearly shows that these states are accessible to solvents and either there description as π– π* core states or their limited excess to the outside environment should be questioned. The effect of solvent on π– π* core state was earlier observed by few researchers in their steady state absorption measurements [9,41] however to the best of our knowledge no one describe it through time resolved emission measurements.

As described above that the emission lifetime of surface group states is independent of excitation wavelength, in order to see whether the lifetime depends on the emitting state or not time resolved emission measurements were carried out by exciting the sample and monitoring the emission at different wavelengths and is shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. S6. The study is important since it gives the information whether emission occur from a single emitting state or from different emitting states. It is interesting to observe from Fig. 7 that as the emission wavelengths increases the emission decay trace becomes slower and lifetime increases. This suggests that the red states are more long lived as compared to blue states. Also the effect is more pronounced in non-polar solvents as compared to polar solvents as can be seen from decay traces in Fig. 7 that when emission is monitored by exciting the CDs at 292 nm, the spread in lifetime is more in chloroform as compared in 2-propanol and Ethylacetate. This has been assigned to the interaction of both π– π* and n– π* states in 2-propanol. Also occurrence of different emission lifetimes suggests that different emission states are involved in emission since it cannot be because of solvent relaxation as if it would have been the reason, the lifetime should be different in different solvents at same emission wavelength and emission position should have changed. However both of these features were not observed (Fig. S3 and Fig. 6, Table S1). This suggests that occurrence of multiple emission sites in surface group states with their individual lifetime is responsible for this excitation dependent emission behavior.

To sum up, the following conclusions can be drawn from above experiments. (1) The CDs are dispersible in both polar and non-polar solvents suggesting their amphiphilic nature, (2) Three different excitation states; π– π*, n– π* and surface group states exist in CDs. In aprotic solvents these states are separated but in protic solvents, both π– π* and n– π* interact. (3) Emission peak position from both π– π* and n– π* is almost independent of excitation wavelength however surface group emission shows excitation dependent emission behavior. (4) The emission lifetime of π– π* > n– π* > surface group states suggesting that the former has enough time to interact with the solvent. The effect is also seen in steady state emission studies where emission peak position changes with change in solvent in this region. (5) Higher growth time while monitoring emission from π– π* state followed by n– π* and surface group states suggests the energy transfer process from former to later. (6) Emission lifetime varies with change in emission wavelength under same excitation in surface group transition states suggesting multiple states exist. This shows that the presence of multiple emission states is the reason for such excitation dependent emission feature in CDs. All these features are summed up and shown in Fig. 8 .
Pallavi Singhal coined the idea, carry out the experiments and written the manuscript. Bal Govind Vats characterize the sample. Vandana Pulhani helped in discussion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: PS and VP would like to acknowledge Dr. A Vinodkumar, and Dr. D. K. Aswal, HS&EG, BARC for their support. BGV would like to acknowledge Dr. S. Kannan, FCD BARC for his support. We acknowledge Dr. A. C Bhasikuttan and Dr. Prabhat K Singh for providing TCSPC facility.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2022.118742.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amphibious fluorescent carbon dots: one-step green synthesis and application for light-emitting polymer nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, L.; He, B.; Huang, J.: Amphibious fluorescent carbon dots: one-step green synthesis and application for light-emitting polymer nanocomposites. Chemical Communications 2013, 49, 8078-8080.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step solvothermal synthesis of high-emissive amphiphilic carbon dots via rigidity derivation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, P.; Li, X.; Baryshnikov, G.; Wu, B.; Agren, H.; Zhang, J.; Zhu, L.: One-step solvothermal synthesis of high-emissive amphiphilic carbon dots via rigidity derivation. Chemical Science 2018, 9, 1323-1329.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green, water-dispersible photoluminescent on–off–on probe for selective detection of fluoride ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singhal, P.; Vats, B. G.; Jha, S. K.; Neogy, S.: Green, Water-Dispersible Photoluminescent On-Off-On Probe for Selective Detection of Fluoride Ions. ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 2017, 9, 20536-20544.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable amphiphilicity and multifunctional applications of ionic-liquid-modified carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, B.; Song, A.; Feng, L.; Ruan, H.; Li, H.; Dong, S.; Hao, J.: Tunable Amphiphilicity and Multifunctional Applications of Ionic-Liquid-Modified Carbon Quantum Dots. ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 2015, 7, 6919-6925.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amphiphilic carbon dots as versatile vectors for nucleic acid and drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, H.-J.; He, X.; Luo, T.-Y.; Zhang, J.; Liu, Y.-H.; Yu, X.-Q.: Amphiphilic carbon dots as versatile vectors for nucleic acid and drug delivery. Nanoscale 2017, 9, 5935-5947.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple microwave-assisted synthesis of amphiphilic carbon quantum dots from A3/B2 polyamidation monomer set

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Choi, Y.; Jo, S.; Chae, A.; Kim, Y. K.; Park, J. E.; Lim, D.; Park, S. Y.; In, I.: Simple Microwave-Assisted Synthesis of Amphiphilic Carbon Quantum Dots from A3/B2 Polyamidation Monomer Set. ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 2017, 9, 27883-27893.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surfactant-derived amphiphilic carbon dots with tunable photoluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kozak, O.; Datta, K. K. R.; Greplova, M.; Ranc, V.; Kaslik, J.; Zboril, R.: Surfactant-Derived Amphiphilic Carbon Dots with Tunable Photoluminescence. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C 2013, 117, 24991-24996.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Origin of excitation dependent fluorescence in carbon nanodots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sharma, A.; Gadly, T.; Gupta, A.; Ballal, A.; Ghosh, S. K.; Kumbhakar, M.: Origin of Excitation Dependent Fluorescence in Carbon Nanodots. The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters 2016, 7, 3695-3702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular origin and self-assembly of fluorescent carbon nanodots in polar solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sharma, A.; Gadly, T.; Neogy, S.; Ghosh, S. K.; Kumbhakar, M.: Molecular Origin and Self-Assembly of Fluorescent Carbon Nanodots in Polar Solvents. The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters 2017, 8, 1044-1052.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exciton delocalization and hot hole extraction in CdSe QDs and CdSe/ZnS type 1 core shell QDs sensitized with newly synthesized thiols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singhal, P.; Ghorpade, P. V.; Shankarling, G. S.; Singhal, N.; Jha, S. K.; Tripathi, R. M.; Ghosh, H. N.: Exciton delocalization and hot hole extraction in CdSe QDs and CdSe/ZnS type 1 core shell QDs sensitized with newly synthesized thiols. Nanoscale 2016, 8, 1823-1833.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrafast hole/electron transfer dynamics in a CdSe quantum dot sensitized by pyrogallol red: a super-sensitization system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singhal, P.; Ghosh, H. N.: Ultrafast Hole/Electron Transfer Dynamics in a CdSe Quantum Dot Sensitized by Pyrogallol Red: A Super-Sensitization System. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C 2014, 118, 16358-16365.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hot-Hole extraction from quantum dot to molecular adsorbate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singhal, P.; Ghosh, H. N.: Hot-Hole Extraction from Quantum Dot to Molecular Adsorbate. Chemistry - A European Journal 2015, 21, 4405-4412.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hot charge carrier extraction from semiconductor quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singhal, P.; Ghosh, H. N.: Hot Charge Carrier Extraction from Semiconductor Quantum Dots. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C 2018, 122, 17586-17600.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron-transfer-mediated uranium detection using quasi-type II core–shell quantum dots: insight into mechanistic pathways

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singhal, P.; Jha, S. K.; Vats, B. G.; Ghosh, H. N.: Electron-Transfer-Mediated Uranium Detection Using Quasi-Type II Core-Shell Quantum Dots: Insight into Mechanistic Pathways. Langmuir 2017, 33, 8114-8122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal–ligand complex-induced ultrafast charge-carrier relaxation and charge-transfer dynamics in CdX (X=S, Se, Te) quantum dots sensitized with nitrocatechol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Singhal, P.; Maity, P.; Jha, S. K.; Ghosh, H. N.: Metal-Ligand Complex-Induced Ultrafast Charge-Carrier Relaxation and Charge-Transfer Dynamics in CdX (X=S, Se, Te) Quantum Dots Sensitized with Nitrocatechol. Chemistry - A European Journal 2017, 23, 10590-10596.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroscopy and femtosecond dynamics of water soluble type I CdSe/ZnS core–shell quantum dot
